16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	
47	
48	\leanok
49	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
50	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
52	  % NOTE (iter-022): CLOSED, axiom-clean {propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}.
53	  % The Lean decl is single-universe `(A B M : Type u)` (was `Type*`): L5 and
54	  % `free_localizationAway_of_away_tower` are single-universe, and the dévissage
55	  % applies them to `C = B ⧸ 𝔭`, forcing A, B into one universe. Faithful for the
56	  % geometric use (scheme patches are single-universe).
57	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
58	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
59	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
60	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
61	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
62	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
63	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
64	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
65	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
66	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
67	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
68	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
69	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.

72	
73	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
74	% exactly this signature):
75	%   (A B M : Type u) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]   % single-universe (iter-022)

81	
82	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
83	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
84	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
85	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
86	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
87	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
88	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
89	% there exists a finite filtration
90	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
91	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
92	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
93	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
94	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
95	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
96	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
97	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
98	\begin{proof}
99	
100	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
101	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
102	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
103	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
104	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
105	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
106	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
107	
108	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
109	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
110	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
111	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
112	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
113	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
114	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
115	  for exactly this residue.
116	
117	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
118	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
119	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
120	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
121	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
122	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
123	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
124	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
125	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
126	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
127	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
128	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
129	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
130	\end{proof}

132	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
133	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
134	
135	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
136	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
137	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
138	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
139	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
140	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
141	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
142	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
143	
144	\begin{lemma}\leanok
145	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
146	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
148	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
149	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
150	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
151	\end{lemma}
152	
153	\begin{proof}
154	
155	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
156	  finitely presented, so
157	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
158	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
159	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
160	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
161	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
162	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
163	  \(f := r\).
164	\end{proof}
165	
166	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
167	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
168	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
169	  \mathlibok
170	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
171	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
172	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
173	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
174	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
175	\end{lemma}
176	
177	\begin{lemma}\leanok
178	[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
179	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
181	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
182	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
183	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
184	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
185	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
186	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
187	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
188	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
189	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
190	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
191	  induction).}
192	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
193	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
194	  \(A_f\).
195	\end{lemma}
196	
197	\begin{proof}
198	
199	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
200	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
201	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
202	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
203	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
204	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
205	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
206	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
207	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
208	\end{proof}
209	
210	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
211	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
212	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
213	  \mathlibok
214	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
215	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
216	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
217	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
218	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
219	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
220	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
221	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
222	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
223	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
224	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{lemma}\leanok
228	[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
229	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
230	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
231	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
232	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
233	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
234	  exact sequence
235	  \[
236	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
237	  \]
238	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular \(i_f\) is injective and \(q_f\) is surjective.
239	\end{lemma}
240	
241	\begin{proof}
242	
243	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
244	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
245	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence.
246	\end{proof}
247	
248	\begin{lemma}\leanok
249	[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
250	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
252	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
253	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
254	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	\begin{proof}
258	
259	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
260	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
261	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
262	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
263	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
264	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
265	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
266	\end{proof}
267	
268	\begin{lemma}\leanok
269	[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
270	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
271	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
272	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
273	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
274	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
275	\end{lemma}
276	
277	\begin{proof}
278	
279	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
280	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
281	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
282	\end{proof}
283	
284	\begin{lemma}\leanok
285	[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
286	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
287	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
288	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
289	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
290	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
291	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
292	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
293	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
294	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
295	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
296	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
297	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
298	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
299	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
300	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
301	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
302	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
303	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
304	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
305	\end{lemma}
306	
307	\begin{proof}
308	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
309	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
310	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
311	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
312	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
313	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
314	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
315	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
316	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
317	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
318	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
319	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
320	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
321	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
322	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
323	  free over \(A_f\).

363	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
364	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0),
365	  %         p ∈ Set.range

375	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
376	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
377	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
378	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
379	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
380	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
381	\end{lemma}
382	
383	\begin{proof}
384	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
385	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
386	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
387	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
388	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
389	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
390	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
391	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
392	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
393	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
394	\end{proof}

467	\begin{proof}
468	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
469	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
470	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
471	  % NOTE: the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a Finset-fold over the finite
472	  % generating set sigma, but each fold step is the Mathlib collapsing lemma
473	  % IsIntegral.exists_multiple_integral_of_isLocalization applied to the generator AS A
474	  % WHOLE (not to its individual coefficient polynomials via gf_clear_one_denominator).
475	  % For each generator x, that lemma yields a single a_x in A^0 (nonzero, since A is a
476	  % domain) with C(a_x) * (1 (x) x) integral over MvPolynomial A; set g1 := prod_x a_x and
477	  % g := g0 * g1. The AlgHom assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
478	
479	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
480	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
481	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
482	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
483	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
484	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
485	
486	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
487	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
488	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
489	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
490	  \(x \in \sigma\) apply the Mathlib collapsing lemma
491	  \texttt{IsIntegral.exists\_multiple\_integral\_of\_isLocalization} (with
492	  \(R = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,A\), \(R_m = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,K\), \(S = B_K\), and the localizing
493	  submonoid the image of \(A^{0}\)): this returns a single
494	  \(a_x \in A^{0}\) (non-zero, as \(A\) is a domain) such that \(C(a_x)\cdot(1\otimes x)\)
495	  is integral over \(\mathrm{MvPoly}\,A = A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
496	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
497	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
498	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
499	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
500	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
501	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the landed proof discharges a whole generator
502	  at once via the collapsing lemma, which folds that primitive over the coefficients
503	  internally.)
504	
505	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
506	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
507	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
508	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
509	
510	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
511	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
512	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
513	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
514	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
515	  \[
516	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
517	  \]
518	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
519	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
520	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
521	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
522	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
523	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
524	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
525	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
526	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
527	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
528	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
529	
530	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
531	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
532	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
533	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
534	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
535	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
536	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
537	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
538	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
539	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
540	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
541	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
542	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
543	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
544	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
545	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
546	
547	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
548	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
549	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
550	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
551	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
552	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
553	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
554	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
555	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
556	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
557	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
558	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
559	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
560	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
561	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
562	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
563	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
564	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
565	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
566	\end{proof}

568	\begin{lemma}\leanok
569	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
570	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
571	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
572	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
573	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
574	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
575	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
576	\end{lemma}
577	\begin{proof}
578	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
579	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
580	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
581	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
582	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
583	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
584	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
585	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
586	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
587	\end{proof}

594	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
595	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
596	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
597	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
598	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
599	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
600	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
601	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
602	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
603	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
604	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
605	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
606	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
607	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
608	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
609	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
610	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
611	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
612	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
613	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
614	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
615	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
616	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
617	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
618	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
619	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
620	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
621	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
622	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
623	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
624	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
625	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
626	  \[
627	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
628	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
629	  \]
630	
631	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
632	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
633	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
634	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
635	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
636	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
637	\end{lemma}
638	
639	\begin{proof}
640	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
641	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
642	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
643	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
644	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
645	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
646	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
647	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
648	
649	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
650	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
651	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
652	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
653	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
654	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
655	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
656	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
657	
658	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
659	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
660	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
661	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
662	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
663	\end{proof}

693	\begin{lemma}\leanok
694	[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
695	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
696	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
697	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
698	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
699	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
700	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
701	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
702	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
703	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
704	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
705	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
706	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
707	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
708	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
709	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
710	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
711	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
712	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
713	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
714	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
715	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
716	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
717	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
718	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
719	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
720	\end{lemma}
721	
722	\begin{proof}
723	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
724	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
725	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
726	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
727	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
728	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
729	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
730	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
731	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
732	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
733	\end{proof}

735	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
736	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
737	
738	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
739	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
740	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
741	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
742	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
743	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
744	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
745	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
746	
747	\begin{definition}\leanok
748	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
749	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
751	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
752	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
753	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
754	  \[
755	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
756	  \]
757	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
758	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
759	\end{definition}
760	
761	\begin{lemma}\leanok
762	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
763	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
764	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
765	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
766	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
767	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
768	\end{lemma}
769	\begin{proof}
770	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
771	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
772	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
773	\end{proof}
774	
775	\begin{definition}\leanok
776	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
777	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
778	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
779	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
780	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
781	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
782	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
783	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
784	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
785	\end{definition}
786	
787	\begin{lemma}\leanok
788	[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
789	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
790	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
791	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
792	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
793	\end{lemma}
794	\begin{proof}
795	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
796	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
797	\end{proof}
798	
799	\begin{lemma}\leanok
800	[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
801	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
802	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
803	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
804	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
805	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
806	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
807	\end{lemma}
808	\begin{proof}
809	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
810	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
811	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
812	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
813	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
814	\end{proof}
815	
816	\begin{lemma}\leanok
817	[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
818	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
819	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
820	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
821	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
822	  transformed monomial is the radix value
823	  \[
824	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
825	  \]
826	\end{lemma}
827	\begin{proof}
828	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
829	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
830	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
831	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
832	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
833	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
834	\end{proof}
835	
836	\begin{lemma}\leanok
837	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
838	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
839	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
840	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
841	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
842	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
843	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
844	\end{lemma}
845	\begin{proof}
846	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
847	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
848	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
849	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
850	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
851	\end{proof}
852	
853	\begin{lemma}\leanok
854	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
855	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
856	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
857	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
858	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
859	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
860	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
861	\end{lemma}
862	\begin{proof}
863	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
864	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
865	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
866	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
867	\end{proof}
868	
869	\begin{lemma}\leanok
870	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
871	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
872	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
873	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
874	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
875	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
876	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
877	\end{lemma}
878	\begin{proof}
879	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
880	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
881	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
882	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
883	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
884	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
885	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
886	\end{proof}
887	
888	\begin{lemma}\leanok
889	[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
890	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
891	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
892	  For any ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and polynomial \(q \in A[X_0, \dots, X_m]\),
893	  the isomorphism \(A[X_0, \dots, X_m] \cong (A[X_1, \dots, X_m])[X_0]\) is natural with
894	  respect to applying \(\varphi\) coefficientwise.
895	\end{lemma}

23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:quot_setup}
25	
26	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
27	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
28	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
29	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
30	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
31	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
32	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
33	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
34	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
35	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
36	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
37	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
38	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
39	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
40	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
41	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
42	
43	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
44	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
45	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
46	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
47	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
48	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
49	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
50	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
51	% reference).
52	
53	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
54	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
55	
56	\begin{definition}\leanok
57	[Hilbert polynomial]
58	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
59	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
60	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
61	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
62	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
63	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
64	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
65	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
66	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
67	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
68	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
69	  % numerical invariant is lost.
70	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
71	  % Polynomials" (read from
72	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
73	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
74	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
75	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
76	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
77	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
78	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
79	  %  the function
80	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
81	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
82	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
83	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
84	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
85	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
86	  %
87	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
88	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
89	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
90	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
91	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
92	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
93	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
94	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
95	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
96	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
97	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
98	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
99	  III.5.2.}
100	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
101	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
102	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
103	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
104	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
105	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
106	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
107	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
108	  \[
109	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
110	  \]
111	  of the section module
112	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
113	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
114	  over the homogeneous section ring
115	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
116	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
117	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
118	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
119	
120	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
121	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
122	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
123	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
124	  \[
125	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
126	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
128	  \]
129	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
130	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
131	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
132	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
133	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
134	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
135	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
136	  well-defined on connected components.
137	\end{definition}

139	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
140	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
141	
142	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
143	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
144	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
145	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
146	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
147	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
148	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
149	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
150	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
151	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
152	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
153	
154	\begin{definition}
155	[Section graded ring]
156	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
157	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
158	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
159	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
160	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
161	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
162	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
163	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
164	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
165	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
166	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
167	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
168	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
169	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
170	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
171	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
172	  %  the function
173	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
174	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
175	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
176	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
177	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
178	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
179	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
180	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
181	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
182	  \[
183	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
184	  \]
185	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
186	  the tensor product of sections
187	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
188	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
189	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
190	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
191	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
192	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
193	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
194	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
195	  polynomial ring).
196	\end{definition}
197	
198	\begin{definition}
199	[Section graded module]
200	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
201	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
202	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
203	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
204	  % Polynomials" (read from
205	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
207	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
208	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
209	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
210	  %  the function
211	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
212	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
213	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
214	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
215	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
216	  I.7.}
217	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
218	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
219	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
220	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
221	  \[
222	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
223	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
224	  \]
225	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
226	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
227	  by the section tensor product
228	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
229	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
230	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
231	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
232	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
233	\end{definition}
234	
235	\begin{lemma}
236	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
237	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
238	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
240	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
241	  % Polynomials" (read from
242	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
243	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
244	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
245	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
246	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
247	  %  the function
248	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
249	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
250	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
251	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
252	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
253	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
254	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
255	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
256	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
257	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
258	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
259	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
260	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
261	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
262	\end{lemma}
263	
264	\begin{proof}
265	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
266	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
267	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
268	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
269	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
270	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
271	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
272	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
273	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
274	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
275	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
276	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
277	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
278	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
279	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
280	\end{proof}
281	
282	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
283	  graded series]
284	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
285	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
286	  \mathlibok
287	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
288	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
289	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
290	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
291	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
292	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
293	  equals \(h(n)\):
294	  \[
295	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
296	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
297	  \]
298	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
299	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
300	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
301	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
302	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
303	\end{lemma}
304	
305	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
306	  (rank--nullity)]
307	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
308	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
309	  \mathlibok
310	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
311	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
312	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
313	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
314	  \[
315	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
316	  \]
317	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
318	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
319	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
320	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
321	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
322	\end{lemma}
323	
324	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
325	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
326	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
327	  \mathlibok
328	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
329	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
330	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
331	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
332	  inverse
333	  \[
334	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
335	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
336	  \]
337	  Together with the coercion
338	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
339	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
340	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
341	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
342	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
343	\end{lemma}
344	
345	\begin{lemma}\leanok
346	[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
347	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
349	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
350	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
351	    lem:ratHilb_bump, def:graded_subquotientHilb,
352	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
353	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
354	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
355	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
356	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
357	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
358	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
359	  %  function
360	  %  $$
361	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
362	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
363	  %  $$
364	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
365	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
366	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
367	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
368	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
369	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
370	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
371	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
372	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
373	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
374	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
375	  such that for all \(n > N\)
376	  \[
377	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
378	  \]
379	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
380	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
381	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
382	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
383	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
384	
385	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
386	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
387	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
388	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
389	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
390	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
391	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
392	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
393	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
394	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
395	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
396	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
397	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
398	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
399	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
